documentCandidates.documentTitle: OPIC Agreements with the Newly Independent States
documentCandidates.context: FROM: Fred M. Zeder J.m.z. President and Chief Executive Officer SUBJECT: OPIC Agreements with the Newly Independent States You will recall that we twice have postponed our Presidential Mission to the former Soviet Union, including the Baltics. The first postponement was due to our success in preliminary Washington negotiations with the U.S.S.R. central government and because the Vice President was able to sign agreements with the Baltic states in Indianapolis. The Mission was postponed again late last year during the politically unsettled time when relations between the center and the republics was highly uncertain. An OPIC technical team visited the major republics instead. Since then, Secretary Baker has signed OPIC bilateral agreements with Armenia and Russia, and our experts have visited all remaining Newly Independent States at his request. We expect

documentCandidates.documentTitle: SUMMARY. IN A SERIES OF MEETINGS WITH DEPARTMENT
documentCandidates.context: SUBJECT: RSFSR FOREIGN MINISTER KOZYREV - DISCUSSES PLANNED YELTSIN VISIT 1. CONFIDENTIAL -- ENTIRE TEXT. 2. SUMMARY. IN A SERIES OF MEETINGS WITH DEPARTMENT OFFICIALS AND NSC STAFF MEMBERS, RSFSR FOREIGN MINISTER KOZYREV CONFIRMED YELTSIN'S PLANS TO VISIT WASHINGTON FROM JUNE 20 TO 23. KOZYREV STRESSED THAT YELTSIN IS ANXIOUS TO AVOID A REPLAY OF HIS LESS-THAN-SUCCESSFUL VISIT TO FRANCE AND EMPHASIZED THAT THE RSFSR GOVERNMENT IS NOT SEEKING TO OBSTRUCT U.S.-SOVIET RELATIONS. documentCandidates.documentTitle: UKRAINE Signing of OPIC Bilateral Agreement
documentCandidates.context: FROM: Fred M. Zeder livil President and Chief Executive Officer SUBJECT: UKRAINE Signing of OPIC Bilateral Agreement We have just completed bilateral negotiations with a Ukrainian delegation led by their Deputy Prime Minister Oleg Slepichev, and last evening we initialed the attached text of their OPIC agreement. The agreement will be ready for signing during the May 6-7 visit to Washington of President Kravchuk. As you recall, during the last summit, President Bush offered OPIC as an element of U.S. assistance to Ukraine that would support and accelerate its successful transition to a market economy. It would be fitting if, on this return visit, President Bush himself would sign the OPIC bilateral agreement with President Kravchuk. A presidential signing would underscore the importance to the United States of a free and independent Ukraine as a major

MEMORANDUM FOR BRENT SCOWCROFT THROUGH: CONDOLEEZZA RICE ER FROM: NICHOLAS BURNS NB SUBJECT: Meeting with Ambassador Jack Matlock Ambassador Jack Matlock will be in Washington for consultations between October 22-26. He has requested a meeting with you and Bob Gates. He has no particular requests to raise but would like the opportunity to review developments in U.S. -Soviet relations. We think a meeting would be worthwhile. Ambassador Matlock is also available to call on the President for a similar discussion but does not want to make a formal request to see him. He would rather leave that decision to you and will understand if a meeting is not possible. The President has not met with Matlock for over a year. A short discussion of events in the USSR, focusing on the economic reform debate and the federation talks, could be useful for the President, should his schedule permit such a meeti

documentCandidates.documentTitle: President Gorbachev's Request for $1.5 Billion in Grain Credit
documentCandidates.context: JUN 4 1991 DECISION MEMORANDUM FOR THE PRESIDENT FROM: by Edward Madigan Secretary SUBJECT: President Gorbachev's Request for $1.5 Billion in Grain Credit Guarantees In our discussion of May 31, 1991, you requested a decision memorandum outlining the four options for responding to President Gorbachev's request. These are given below. My recommendation is that you select Option 2, provision of the full $1.5 billion requested in tranches with appropriate assurances. Possible Options Option 1: Provide $1.5 billion in GSM credit guarantees without conditions. Arguments for: You provide President Gorbachev with material evidence of your support, evidence which he seeks. His personal guarantee increases the likelihood that this debt would be repaid. The Soviets have not been late on official debt payments to Western

documentCandidates.documentTitle: Draft Presidential Letter to French President
documentCandidates.context: April 28, 1992 CONF DENT MEMORANDUM FOR BRENT SCOWCROFT THE WHITE HOUSE Subject: Draft Presidential Letter to French President Francois Mitterrand on His Request for Support for a Foundation to Employ Non-Defense Scientists from the Former Soviet Union This memorandum transmits a draft letter for the President's signature in response to an April 15 letter from French President Mitterrand. President Mitterrand commends the concept of the Moscow-based International Science and Technology Center which is currently being established through the efforts of the U.S., Russia, Japan and the European Community, and which will provide civilian work for weapons scientists and engineers from the former Soviet Union. He asks for support for the creation of an international foundation directed at employing ex-Soviet scientists from all the fields

FROM: Dennis K. Dolan Corporate Secretary and Executive Staff Assistant SUBJECT: Draft Presidential Letters I have attached a draft of three proposed letters that OPIC President and C.E.O., Fred M. Zeder, would like to present to the heads of state of Estonia, Latvia and Lithuania when he visits the Baltics as chairman of the OPIC investment mission. Ambassador Zeder departs for the Baltics August 21. We continue to appreciate the efforts you give to our requests. If you have any questions or need additional information, do not hesitate to contact me at 336-8403. Peter Ballinger, manager of the OPIC Investment Missions Program, will contact your office in mid-August to check on the status of the letters. Thank you for your assistance.

September 30, 1989 LIMITED OFFICIAL USE MEMORANDUM FOR BRENT SCOWCROFT THE WHITE HOUSE Subject: U.S.-Soviet Ministerial in Wyoming, September 21-24: Agreements Signed Secretary Baker and Soviet Foreign Minister Shevardnadze signed several agreements at the recently concluded Ministerial in Jackson Hole, Wyoming. Copies of those agreements, in English and in Russian, are attached to this memorandum. They include: (1) Memorandum of Understanding on Chemical Weapons; (2) Agreement on Notification of Major Strategic Exercises; (3) Umbrella Verification Agreement; (4) Agreement on Bering Straits Visa Free Travel; (5) Agreement on Bering Straits Regional Commission, and (6) Joint Statement on Law of the Sea/Innocent Passage. ACTION MEMORANDUM FOR THE PRESIDENT FROM: BRENT SCOWCROFT SUBJECT: Letter to Cypriot President Vassiliou Purpose To respond to two recent letters from President George Vassiliou of Cyprus Background Cypriot President George Vassiliou wrote to you on May 17 requesting that you discuss the Cyprus problem with President Gorbachev at the Summit. He wrote to you again on June 5 thanking you for having raised the issue with Gorbachev and suggesting that he would appreciate further discussions of Cyprus in your future meetings with the Soviet leader. documentCandidates.documentTitle: Request for Designation as Presidential Mission
documentCandidates.context: March 18, 1992 DECL: OADR MEMORANDUM FOR BRENT SCOWCROFT THE WHITE HOUSE Subject: Request for Designation as Presidential Mission The Russian government has accepted the U.S. proposal to hold a first session of the U.S.-Russian joint commission on POWs/MIAs March 26-28 in Moscow. Malcolm Toon, former Ambassador to the Soviet Union, has been selected to chair the U.S. delegation to the joint commission. Toon will be joined as commission members by two State Department Deputy Assistant Secretaries, Richard Kauzlarich of European Affairs and Kenneth Quinn of East Asian Affairs. Representing other agencies will be DAS Alan Ptak of the Defense Department's Office of POW-MIA Affairs and DIA Chief of Staff Denis Clift. From Congress, Senators Kerry and Smith By SS NARA, Date 12/12/25 PER DOS WAIVER, November 6, 2015

documentCandidates.documentTitle: Presidential Message for the Moscow Conference on Law
documentCandidates.context: August 31, 1990 LIMITED OFFICIAL USE MEMORANDUM FOR BRENT SCOWCROFT THE WHITE HOUSE Subject: Presidential Message for the Moscow Conference on Law and Bilateral Economic Relations The Moscow Conference on Law and Bilateral Economic Relations will be held September 19-21. Sponsored by the Union of Soviet Lawyers, the USSR Ministry of Justice, the USSR State Commission on Foreign Economic Relations and the Seattle-based Center for International Cooperation, the conference expects to welcome 600 American and 900 Soviet lawyers. Former Secretary of State William Rogers is the chairman of the U.S. Organizing Committee. Anatoliy Luk'yanov, Chairman of the Supreme Soviet of the USSR, is expected to address the conference. The President has been requested to provide a Special Message to the Conference. We have attached a draft reply from the President.

documentCandidates.documentTitle: Trade and Investment Issues with the USSR
documentCandidates.context: UNITED STATES OF AMERICA 5278 July 12, 1991 MEMORANDUM FOR BRENT SCOWCROFT SUBJECT: Trade and Investment Issues with the USSR Following up on our conversation last night, I wanted to share some of my thoughts in writing. The business environment is highly unstable. The center, the republics, and the localities all argue over the division of authority and control over national assets. Laws contradict each other and constantly change. Nobody is willing to make decisions and stick by them. President Gorbachev and the republic leaders need to focus on the American investment projects that have been stymied by Soviet indecision. If they can break the bottlenecks on these projects, U.S. private investment would begin to flow almost immediately. Paying their bad debts to U.S. companies would also generate more confidence.

MEMORANDUM FOR THE PRESIDENT THROUGH: General Scowcroft FROM: Nicholas F. Brady 27B SUBJECT: Soviet Participation in the IMF At the meeting of the G-7 Finance Deputies on Saturday December 14, there was an initial discussion of possible membership in the IMF by the Soviet Republics. It is clear that the views of the Finance Ministries have moved quite far in light of recent developments and that a general consensus has emerged that early membership by the Republics would be desirable and inevitable. I understand that Prime Minister Major may have written to the Summit Heads of State over the weekend urging membership for the Russian Republic by this spring. The G-7 Deputies agreed that Republic membership would provide a useful means of promoting economic reform and the resources needed to help assure that reforms are fully implemented and succeed. documentCandidates.documentTitle: SOVIET LEADERSHIP CONDOLENCES ON SAN FRANCISCO
documentCandidates.context: UNCLAS MOSCOW 29079 Brux Thanke em E.O. 12356: N/A TAGS: PREL, SENV, CASC, UR, US SUBJECT: SOVIET LEADERSHIP CONDOLENCES ON SAN FRANCISCO EARTHQUAKE 1. MF A USA ADMINISTRATION CHIEF OBUKHOV CALLED AMBASSADOR MORNING OCTOBER 18 TO PASS CONDOLENCES FROM GORBACHEV AND SHEVARDNADZE ON THE SAN FRANCISCO 68/18 EARTHQUAKE. OBUKHOV SAID BOTH WERE BEING KEPT CLOSELY INFORMED ON THE SITUATION. AND WOULD BE SENDING MORE FORMAL STATEMENTS OF SYMPATHY TO APPROPRIATE OFFICIALS IN WASHINGTON. 2. SHEVARDNADZE ASKED THAT THE SECRETARY BE INFORMED THAT THE SOVIET UNION STOOD READY TO HELP WHENEVER AND HOWEVER IT MIGHT BE OF ASSISTANCE. 3. OBUKHOV ALSO SAID THAT HE HAD RECEIVED A MESSAGE

documentCandidates.documentTitle: Report on Federal Railroad Administration Delegation's
documentCandidates.context: Ground Floor, West Wing The White House Washington, D. C. 20500 Dear Mr. Hughes: Subject: Report on Federal Railroad Administration Delegation's Visit to Soviet Union, September 24-30, 1989 The unique feature of the Soviet's railway system is that, unlike any other industry in that country, including even agriculture, it encompasses the length and breadth of their land mass. It hauls four times as much freight as U.S. railroads, moves eight billion passengers annually, and employs four million people, or almost two percent of the population of the Soviet Union. One of its representatives referred to the Rail Ministry as a state within a state--an apt description. It is literally the freight and passenger transportation lifeline for the Soviet Union since highways are few and far between. documentCandidates.documentTitle: Request for a Meeting with President Bush for the
documentCandidates.context: CONFIDENTIAL (with CONFIDENTIAL attachment) MEMORANDUM FOR BRENT SCOWCROFT THE WHITE HOUSE Subject: Request for a Meeting with President Bush for the Estonian Prime Minister The Department recommends that the President receive Prime Minister Edgar Savisaar of Estonia who will be in the United States on an unofficial visit October 3-10, accompanied by Foreign Minister Lennart Meri. The Estonian government has requested this opportunity to meet with the President and discuss its efforts to achieve a peaceful, negotiated solution to Baltic self-determination. We believe that such a meeting would be very helpful in encouraging the Baltic dialogue, and could be handled on the same basis as the previous meetings with the Lithuanian and Latvian Prime Ministers. for J. Stapleton Roy

, 1991 Presidential Determination No. MEMORANDUM FOR THE SECRETARY OF STATE SUBJECT: Determination under Subsection 2 (b) (2) of the Export-Import Bank Act of 1945, as Amended -- Latvia, Lithuania, and Estonia Pursuant to Subsection 2 (b) (2) of the Export-Import Bank Act of 1945, as amended, I determine that it is in the national interest for the Export-Import Bank of the United States to guarantee, insure, extend credit and participate in the extension of credit in connection with the purchase or lease of any product of service by, for use in, or for sale or lease to the Baltic States of Latvia, Lithuania, and Estonia. On my behalf, please transmit this determination to the Speaker of the House and to the President of the Senate. This determination shall be published in the Federal Register.

documentCandidates.documentTitle: Aircraft Request for OPIC Delegation to the N.I.S.
documentCandidates.context: E.vil FROM: Fred M. Zeder President and Chief Executive Officer SUBJECT: Aircraft Request for OPIC Delegation to the N.I.S. Attached is a memorandum to General Scowcroft proposing that OPIC's Presidential Mission to the Newly Independent States of the former Soviet Union, originally scheduled for last Fall, be rescheduled for the period from June 10 through 20. The purpose of the Mission would be to sign bilateral agreements on behalf of the United States with several of the N.I.S. governments to enable OPIC to support U.S. private investment in these countries. As before, the logistical problems are extremely difficult, given the short time we will be in each city and the unreliability of commercial transportation. Therefore, we once again request a government aircraft for this Mission, as previously approved for its earlier schedule.

documentCandidates.documentTitle: Presidential Missions to Romanian and Bulgarian
documentCandidates.context: May 4, 1990 CONFIDENTIAL MEMORANDUM FOR BRENT SCOWCROFT THE WHITE HOUSE Subject: Presidential Missions to Romanian and Bulgarian Elections The U.S. has a high stake in ensuring that the national elections in Romania (May 20) and Bulgaria (June 10, and runoff on June 17) are free and fair. Both publicly and privately, we have indicated that the freedom and fairness of upcoming elections will go far in determining our policies and assistance to each of the countries in Eastern Europe and the Soviet Union. Speaking for the Administration, Secretary Baker has called for observers from the U.S. and other CSCE states at the elections. Romania and Bulgaria have expressed willingness to facilitate activities by election observers. High visibility Presidential Missions to Romania and Bulgaria would make a difference in the electoral campaigns

documentCandidates.context: LIMITED OFFICIAL USE August 27, 1990 MEMORANDUM FOR BRENT SCOWCROFT THE WHITE HOUSE Subject: Message from the Soviet-American Women's Summit On July 27, Deputy Assistant Secretary of State for European and Canadian Affairs Curtis W. Kamman met with six representatives of the Soviet-American Women's Summit, a group of over 100 U.S. and Soviet women from diverse backgrounds who met prior to the recent summit meeting between Presidents Bush and Gorbachev. At the Soviet-American Women's Summit, the delegates agreed by consensus to a message to the Presidents containing eight proposals which they hoped the Presidents would keep in mind during their discussions and would implement on behalf of their two nations. December 9, 1990 CONF IDENT EXDIS MEMORANDUM FOR BRENT SCOWCROFT THE WHITE HOUSE SUBJECT: Presidential Call to President Mitterand During negotiations December 8 in the Security Council, the French were extremely unhelpful, in the end abstaining on the Soviet motion to defer a vote -- the motion passed by the barest possible positive vote (9-4-2). The Gang of Four opposed, and France and China abstained. The Secretary has had considerable discussions with Roland Dumas on this subject and, given the similarity of talking points by their perm rep and Ambassador Andreani, the Secretary believes it will be necessary for the President to talk directly with Mitterand if French policy is to be turned around. documentCandidates.documentTitle: Appointment Request: NATO Secretary General Manfred
documentCandidates.context: (with SECRET attachment) DECL: OADR MEMORANDUM FOR BRENT SCOWCROFT THE WHITE HOUSE SUBJECT: Appointment Request: NATO Secretary General Manfred Woerner, October 8-12, 1991 NATO Secretary General Manfred Woerner will be in Washington October 8-12. The Department recommends the President meet with Woerner during his stay here and, if possible, host a small lunch for him. Woerner's visit to Washington will be particularly timely, coming a month before the November 7-8 NATO Summit in Rome. A meeting with Woerner, one of our most articulate supporters in the Alliance, will offer the President a chance to review objectives and plans for the Summit, which will address pivotal issues of European security identity and NATO's future role, including NATO's Strategy Review, Alliance relations with the Soviet Union and Central and Eastern Europe, CSCE and arms

documentCandidates.documentTitle: Proposed Board Members for the Eurasia Foundation
documentCandidates.context: WASHINGTON May 6, 1992 MEMORANDUM TO THE ASSISTANT TO THE PRESIDENT FOR NATIONAL SECURITY AFFAIRS SUBJECT: Proposed Board Members for the Eurasia Foundation As you may recall, one of the assistance initiatives that we launched at the Washington Coordinating Conference for the New Independent States was the creation of the Eurasia Foundation. The Eurasia Foundation is designed to promote and strengthen market economies and institutions concerned with representative government, effective legal systems, human rights, and responsible media. The Foundation will be uniquely equipped to provide immediate, quick-start) on-the-ground assistance throughout the Independent States, in partnership with American private Bush Presidential Library Photocopy voluntary and non-governmental organizations. SUMMARY CHAIRMAN OF THE HOUSE AGRICULTURAL OF FARMERS AND OTHER AGRICULTURAL SPECIALISTS is A GOOD COMMITTEE, E. "KIKA" DE LA GARZA, LED A DELEGATION OF SIX IDEA AND THAT THIS IS ALREADY BEING DONE. HE ALSO U.S. CONGRESSMEN TO THE SOVIET UNION FROM AUGUST 22-26. VISITED SEVERAL LEADING FARM ENTERPRISES. THE DELEGATION

ACTION MEMORANDUM FOR THE PRESIDENT FROM: BRENT SCOWCROFT SUBJECT: Thank You to Gorbachev Purpose The note thanks Gorbachev for his message of condolence and offer to help in the wake of the San Francisco Bay Area earthquake. Background You received a condolence message from Gorbachev and an offer to help the victims of the San Francisco earthquake. Ambassador Dubinin was asked to personally convey the Soviet leader's sympathies to you. RECOMMENDATION That you sign the message to Gorbachev at Tab A. Attachment Tab A Message to Gorbachev

documentCandidates.documentTitle: Appointment Request: FRG Foreign Minister Genscher
documentCandidates.context: November 9, 1989 CONF IDENTIAL MEMORANDUM FOR BRENT SCOWCROFT THE WHITE HOUSE Subject: Appointment Request: FRG Foreign Minister Genscher FRG Foreign Minister Hans-Dietrich Genscher will visit Washington November 21, and has requested a meeting with the President followed by a meeting with you. Genscher plans to meet separately with Secretary Baker. Genscher's main focus will be the President's upcoming meeting with Soviet President Gorbachev. In this context, he will be interested in discussing the latest developments in East Germany and in Eastern Europe. Executive J. Stapleton Itapleton Secretary Roy Pay Attachment: Tab 1 - - Biographic information. CONF IDENTIAL DECL: OADR

November 9, 1989 CONFIDENTIAL MEMORANDUM FOR BRENT SCOWCROFT THE WHITE HOUSE Subject: Cypriot President Requests US-Soviet Statement on Cyprus Cypriot President Vassiliou has written to the President asking that he and Soviet Chairman Gorbachev issue a statement on Cyprus at their December meeting in the Mediterranean. We understand that Vassiliou is sending a similar request to Chairman Gorbachev. We have repeatedly told the Cypriots that we do not believe Cyprus belongs on the Soviet-American agenda. The Soviets have nothing positive to offer, their interests on the island compete with ours, and we do not want to engage them in a dispute that involves two NATO allies, Greece and Turkey. The Soviets make half-hearted efforts to discuss Cyprus at high level meetings to be able to tell the Cypriots that they

documentCandidates.documentTitle: French Adherence to the Nuclear Non-Proliferation
documentCandidates.context: CONFIDENTIAL 3740 MAY 10 1990 MEMORANDUM FOR THE PRESIDENT SUBJECT: French Adherence to the Nuclear Non-Proliferation Treaty (U) President de Gaulle decided in 1968 that France would not become a party to the Nuclear Non-Proliferation Treaty (NPT) primarily out of pique for not having been a central participant in the negotiation of the Treaty. Nevertheless, France has for the past 20 years been following a declared policy of behaving largely as if it were a party. France is now actively considering whether to reverse its anomalous policy of non-adherence to the NPT. The attached draft letter from you to President Mitterand welcomes this consideration and urges a decision in favor of adherence. ($) Because the NPT plays a central role in pursuing our nuclear

CONF IDENTIAL January 22, 1991 MEMORANDUM FOR BRENT SCOWCROFT THE WHITE HOUSE Subject: Presidential Proclamation Commemorating Bicentennial of U.S. -Portuguese Relations; Presidential letter to Portuguese President Soares On February 21, 1791, Congress approved the President's nomination of our first diplomatic representative to Portugal. Senator Pell and Rep. Machtley plan to introduce a Joint Resolution of Congress to commemorate the bicentennial of U.S. -Portuguese diplomatic relations. The Department has solicited testimonials from past U.S. Ambassadors, which will be presented to the Portuguese MFA. Embassy Lisbon will unveil a plaque commemorating the establishment of our diplomatic relations and Ambassador Briggs plans to host a reception. documentCandidates.context: JULY 24, 1991 MEMORANDUM FOR GENERAL BRENT SCOWCROFT FROM: AMBASSADOR CARLA A. HILLS CAST SUBJECT: THE PRESIDENT'S DECISION TO TRANSMIT THE AGREEMENT ON TRADE RELATIONS BETWEEN THE UNITED STATES OF AMERICA AND THE UNION OF SOVIET SOCIALIST REPUBLICS TO THE CONGRESS I have now had a chance to review the assurances that were given by Foreign Minister Alexander Bessmertnykh to Secretary of State Baker last week. I understand that the Foreign Minister promised that the Soviet Union would make every effort to bring Soviet laws into conformity with their commitments under the Trade Agreement. This commitment was reiterated to an interagency team that went to the Soviet Union last week to review Soviet intellectual property legislation. documentCandidates.documentTitle: Draft Presidential Greeting Letter for Meeting
documentCandidates.context: October 28, 1991 UNCLASSIFIED MEMORANDUM FOR BRENT SCOWCROFT THE WHITE HOUSE SUBJECT: Draft Presidential Greeting Letter for Meeting Hosted by Frank Carlucci and Sir Geoffrey Howe In a letter dated October 18, 1991, H. Eugene Douglas requested that the President send a welcoming message to a meeting of a distinguished group of Americans and Europeans to be read at the opening session on October 29. Sponsored by the West-West Agenda and the American Academy of Diplomacy, the meeting will discuss the topic of "West-West Relations in Light of Developments in the Soviet Union, Eastern Europe, and the Middle East." The hosts are: for the American delegation, Frank Carlucci, and for the European delegation, Sir Geoffrey Howe. Attached is a draft welcoming statement, addressed to Frank Carlucci.

documentCandidates.documentTitle: Conversion of Defense Industries in the Former
documentCandidates.context: WASHINGTON, DC 20301-1000 UNITED STATES OF AMERICA 6 NOV 1991 MEMORANDUM FOR THE PRESIDENT SUBJECT: Conversion of Defense Industries in the Former Soviet Union Deputy Secretary Atwood is currently in the former Soviet Union, heading up a delegation of DoD personnel and private sector industrialists. The purpose of their visit is to evaluate conversion potential and processes, and to discuss related matters with appropriate military, political and industrial officials. Due to the importance of the issues involved, I request that you meet with this group (7-10 people) sometime after their return to the United States. The delegation will return on 6 November. I would envision a meeting of no more than one hour duration. De Change

documentCandidates.documentTitle: Ratification of the Convention for the Conservation
documentCandidates.context: Washington, D.C. 20520 October 1, 1992 MEMORANDUM FOR BRENT SCOWCROFT THE WHITE HOUSE Subject: Ratification of the Convention for the Conservation of Anadromous Stocks in the North Pacific Ocean, with Annex Attached for signature by the President is the instrument of ratification, in duplicate, of the Convention for the Conservation of Anadromous Stocks in the North Pacific Ocean, with Annex, signed at Moscow February 11, 1992, by the United States, Canada, Japan, and the Russian Federation. The purpose of the Convention is to prohibit high seas fishing for Pacific salmon. It also establishes a new organization, the North Pacific Anadromous Fish Commission (NPAFC), which will contribute significantly to the conservation of anadromous fishery resources and ecologically related species.

PER NSC WAIVER, 1500 2021-02 By SS NARA, Date 9/24/25 CONFIDENTIAL Declassify on: OADR SUBJECT: Soviet Emigration Bill I think that our policy of withholding credits and MFN toward the Soviet Union pending the passage of the Emigration Bill needs to be reviewed with an eye to modification. Even if the Soviet Emigration bill were passed, it is my understanding that benefits from us would only be extended on an annual basis, subject to review on performance criteria. As we will be making annual decisions in any case, what matters most is the de facto, not the de jure situation with regard to Jewish emigration. At this point, we are happy with the de facto situation regarding Jewish emigration. However, it is not at all clear to me that the Soviet Union will agree to an Emigration Bill satisfactory to us. BEREND-JAN BARON VAN VOORST TOT VOBRST (CDA), CURRENTLY STATE SECRETARY FOR EUROPEAN AFFAIRS IN THE MFA, WILL BE THE NEW STATE SECRETARY FOR DEFENSE. AFTER HIS SEPTEMBER 1988 VISIT TO WASHINGTON IN CONNECTION WITH HIS CANDIDACY FOR THE CHAIRMANSHIP OF COCOM, VAN VOORST IS KNOWN TO MANY WASHINGTON POLICY MAKERS. VAN VOORST, WHO WILL PROBABLY CONCERN HIMSELF WITH MATERIEL POLICY, HAS NO SPECIFIC KNOWLEDGE OF DEFENSE, but HIS EUROPEAN EXPERIENCE WILL LIKELY COME IN HANDY WHEN HE GETS INVOLVED IN THE EUROPEANIZATION OF ARMS PRODUCTION. documentCandidates.documentTitle: SUMMARY: UNITED BERLIN'S GOVERNING MAYOR, EBERHARD
documentCandidates.context: REFS: (A) BERLIN 191; (B) BERLIN 518 WARD 1. ENTIRE TEXT. 2. SUMMARY: UNITED BERLIN'S GOVERNING MAYOR, EBERHARD DIEPGEN (CDU), WOULD LIKE TO MAKE HIS FIRST MAJOR FOREIGN TRIP TO WASHINGTON. AFTER BEING PRESSED BY THE SOVIETS, HE PLANS TO TRAVEL TO MOSCOW MARCH 26-28. HE WOULD CARRY TO THE PRESIDENT AND SECRETARY BERLIN'S SPECIAL THANKS FOR OUR STEADFAST SUPPORT FOR THE CITY'S FREEDOM AND UNITY AS WELL AS A MESSAGE OF BERLINERS' AND GERMANS' LIDARITY ON THE GULF. HIS PLAN HAS CHANCELLOR KOHL'S SUPPORT. A DIEPGEN VISIT WOULD ALSO PROVIDE U.S. POLICYMAKERS WITH A FIRST HAND ACCOUNT OF THE PROBLEMS IN MELDING THE OLD AND NEW GERMAN STATES. IF DIEPGEN'S TIMING FITS (PARA 31, I WOULD SUGGEST A BRIEF PHOTO OPPORTUNITY WITH THE PRESIDENT AND A LONGER CONVERSATION WITH THE SECRETARY AS WELL AS OTHER MEETINGS IN THE DEPARTMENT. THE GERMAN EMBASSY

Suite 417 Washington, D.C. 20036 202-835-0060 MEMORANDUM To: Those interested in the Sokolov/Samarin Case From: The Congress of Russian Americans Washington Office Subject: Current Standing of the Samarin Case Date: September 1, 1988 The present situation: The Supreme Court of the United States turned down Mr. Sokolov's peti- tion. This means that the United States Court of Appeals, Second Circuit decision of March 24, 1987 stands. Therefore, his US citizenship has been revoked. Deportation hearings have been started against him even though he has not been personally served. There are three avenues open to him. The first is a Presidential or Attorney General intervention; the second is a private bill passed by congress; the third 1s to find a friendly country that will allow him to reside there. documentCandidates.context: UNCLAS SECTION 01 OF 02 MOSCOW 12309 DEPT FOR EUR/SOV E.0. 12356. N/A TAGS: PREL. ZB SUBJECT: LITHUANIAN WOMEN'S LETTER TO MRS. BUSH 1. ON APRIL 11, EMBOFF MET FOR A HALF HOUR WITH A DELEGATION OF NINE LITHUANIAN WOMEN, INCLUDING A FORMER USSR PEOPLE'S DEPUTY. A LITHUANIAN SUPREME COUNCIL MEMBER, AND REPRESENTATIVES OF OTHER ORGANIZATIONS. THE WOMEN HAD REQUESTED THE MEETING TO DELIVER A LETTER FOR TRANSMITTAL TO MRS. BARBARA BUSH. EMBOFF UNDERTOOK TO TRANSMIT THE LETTER AND INFORM THEM OF ANY RESPONSE, WITHOUT PROMISING ONE. THE TEXT OF THE LETTER (WHICH WAS WRITTEN IN ENGLISH) FOLLOWS IN PARA 4, BELOW. THE ORIGINAL IS BEING POUCHED UNDER REGISTRY NO. 6086520 TO EUR/SOV FOR TRANSMITTAL. 2. BEFORE PASSING ON THE LETTER, THE WOMEN: - - EXPRESSED THANKS FOR THE UNITED STATES' MORAL SUPPORT

documentCandidates.documentTitle: Suggested Reply to the Mayor of Seattle
documentCandidates.context: December 20, 1989 UNCLASSIFIED MEMORANDUM FOR BRENT SCOWCROFT THE WHITE HOUSE Subject: Suggested Reply to the Mayor of Seattle The Mayor of Seattle Charles Royer and Mayor-Elect Norman B. Rice have written to the President requesting that he endorse the creation of a U.S.-Soviet Peace Corps. The U.S. Ploughshares for Peace organization and the Soviet Peace Fund are co-sponsoring a "U.S.-Soviet Partners Project," under which plans are being made for sending American and Soviet volunteer teams to provide humanitarian aid to Ethiopia. Mayor Royer believes that the U.S.-Soviet Partners project could serve as a model for a U.S.-Soviet Peace Corps. Attached please find the Department of State's draft response to Mayor Royer for the President's signature. hid Klem for J. Stapleton Roy

September 15, 1990 UNCLASSIFIED MEMORANDUM FOR BRENT SCOWCROFT THE WHITE HOUSE Subject: Baltic Freedom Report The Department recommends that the President submit the attached "Baltic Freedom Report" to Congress, as requested in Public Law 101-309 of June 18, 1990. That law, a Joint Resolution of Congress, designates June 14 as "Baltic Freedom Day" and calls on the President to submit to Congress within 60 days a statement of specific actions which the United States Government is taking to support the peaceful restoration of the independence of the Baltic States. The attached statement, entitled "Baltic Freedom Report," summarizes the Administration's successful efforts since March to promote a peaceful negotiated process to ensure Baltic freedom.

documentCandidates.documentTitle: Paris CSCE Summit: Proposed Delegation List
documentCandidates.context: Washington, D.C. 20520 November 2, 1990 MEMORANDUM FOR BRENT SCOWCROFT THE WHITE HOUSE Subject: Paris CSCE Summit: Proposed Delegation List The Department offers for your consideration the attached suggested delegation list for U.S. attendance at the Paris CSCE Summit. In addition to the names on this list, we also recommend that CFE Ambassador Woolsey be invited to Paris to take part in the CFE signing ceremony. The list is based in part on participation in the last CSCE Summit, held on the occasion of the signing of the Helsinki Final Act in 1975. The French have informed us that each country will be allowed seating for a total of 30 delegates and "guests" at any one time, divided among three different conference halls in the Kleber Center. This is not a limit on the size of the delegation itself, which is a White House prerogative, but rather on the number of people we can have

MEMORANDUM TO THE PRESIDENT FROM: Clayton Yeutter Secretary of Agriculture SUBJECT: Soviet Export Credit Guarantees Background: World wheat production is at a record high this year. Our own wheat production doubled from the drought reduced 1989 crop. Soviet production is estimated to be record high as well. The Soviet shortage of foreign exchange has set off intense global competition for sales to that market. All major exporters other than the U.S. have offered special discounts and/or credits to the Soviets in order to book sales. The United States has offered prices that are competitive with the European Community through our Export Enhancement Program (EEP). However, the Soviets have stated that they will not purchase additional grain without credit guarantees. They have further stated that refusal of credit guarantees makes our prices uncompetitive and, therefore, `even the minimum

documentCandidates.documentTitle: Meeting Request with the President: Wallenberg's Sister
documentCandidates.context: September 29, 1989 CONFIDENTIAL MEMORANDUM FOR BRENT SCOWCROFT THE WHITE HOUSE Subject: Meeting Request with the President: Wallenberg's Sister Nina Lagergren, Raoul Wallenberg's sister, has requested a meeting with the President during her visit in Washington, October 2 or 3. Mrs. Lagergren and three others have been invited by Soviet officials to talks in Moscow in mid-October to discuss the fate of her brother, Raoul. Her unprecedented meeting with Soviet officials to discuss this sensitive issue comes soon after October 5, proclaimed "Raoul Wallenberg Day" in a congressional joint resolution. There is great congressional interest in the fate of this brave man who has come to symbolize courage in the face of Nazism and Stalinism, and whom President Reagan proclaimed an honorary American citizen in 1981. We recommend the President meet with Mrs. Lagergren.

October 13, 1989 CONF IDENTIAL MEMORANDUM FOR BRENT SCOWCROFT THE WHITE HOUSE Subject: U.S.-Soviet Maritime Boundary At the margins of the U.S.-Soviet ministerial, we met September 19 with Soviet Legal Adviser Yuriy Rybakov to seek to resolve outstanding issues on the maritime boundary. documentCandidates.documentTitle: Twenty-Seventh Semiannual Report to the CSCE Commission
documentCandidates.context: 94E5 November 21, 1989 MEMORANDUM FOR BRENT SCOWCROFT THE WHITE HOUSE SUBJECT: Twenty-Seventh Semiannual Report to the CSCE Commission Public Law 94-304 requires the President to submit a report semiannually to the Congressional CSCE Commission on the implementation of the Helsinki Final Act. The attached draft report, which covers the period April 1 - September 30, 1989, is due to the Commission on December 3. The report provides a factual survey of developments in the areas covered by the Helsinki Final Act and the Madrid and Vienna Concluding Documents: human rights and humanitarian concerns; security; economic, scientific and technological cooperation; and educational and cultural exchanges. It concentrates on compliance with the Final Act and the Concluding Documents by the Soviet Union and the countries of Eastern Europe. The Department recommends that the Secretary of State be

documentCandidates.documentTitle: Appointment Request for Irish Foreign Minister Gerard
documentCandidates.context: (With SECRET Attachment) December 7, 1989 MEMORANDUM FOR BRENT SCOWCROFT THE WHITE HOUSE Subject: Appointment Request for Irish Foreign Minister Gerard Collins On January 1, Ireland will assume its turn as President of the EC at a time when the EC is removing remaining barriers among its members and responding to changes in the Soviet Union and Eastern Europe. When Prime Minister Haughey called on President Bush last St. Patrick's Day, he promised that Ireland would use its EC presidency to oppose European protectionism and to resolve any problems which might arise between the U.S. and the EC. On January 12, Foreign Minister Collins - following the custom for EC Presidency Foreign Ministers - will visit Washington to discuss his country's EC presidency with Secretary Baker and other senior officials. documentCandidates.documentTitle: Appointment Request for Norwegian Prime Minister
documentCandidates.context: January 19, 1990 CONF TAL MEMORANDUM FOR BRENT SCOWCROFT THE WHITE HOUSE Subject: Appointment Request for Norwegian Prime Minister Jan P. Syse The new Norwegian Prime Minister has asked to meet with the President during a visit to Washington, which the Norwegians would like to schedule for February 22-23, 1990. Prime Minister Syse took office October 16. Though Syse's coalition is deeply divided over economic and social policies, it has survived tough budget negotiations and will probably hold together for the foreseeable future. Lacking previous experience in foreign affairs, Syse wants to build his credentials as a world leader with close personal ties to Norway's most important ally. documentCandidates.context: UNCLASSIFIED January 5, 1990 MEMORANDUM FOR BRENT SCOWCROFT THE WHITE HOUSE Subject: Suggested Reply to University of Washington Regarding a Proposed U.S.-Soviet International Development Corps The Assistant Director of the University of Washington's Foreign Studies Office, David M. Fenner, has written to you about a joint proposal by the U.S. Ploughshares for Peace organization and the Soviet Peace Fund to create an international development corps which would include Soviets and Americans. Attached at Tab 1 please find the Department of State's draft response to Mr. Fenner for your signature. (With SECRET Attachment) MEMORANDUM FOR BRENT SCOWCROFT THE WHITE HOUSE SUBJECT: Appointment of His Excellency Aleksandr Aleksandrovich Bessmertnykh of the Union of Soviet Socialist Republics as Ambassador to the United States In a meeting today with the Deputy Secretary, Ambassador Dubinin verbally inquired whether our Government would agree to the appointment of Aleksandr Aleksandrovich Bessmertnykh to succeed him as Ambassador Extraordinary and Plenipotentiary of the Union of Soviet Socialist Republics to the United States. A biography of Mr. Bessmertnykh is attached. The Department believes from the information available that Mr. Bessmertnykh will make an excellent Ambassador to the United States and recommends that the President agree to the proposed appointment. documentCandidates.documentTitle: Briefing the President for the U.S. -Soviet Summit
documentCandidates.context: MEMORANDUM FOR BRENT SCOWCROFT THROUGH: ROBERT D. BLACKWILL RB FROM: NICHOLAS BURNS NB SUBJECT: Briefing the President for the U.S. -Soviet Summit The President has agreed to five, one-hour briefings on the U.S.- Soviet Summit between May 23-30, and times have been set aside by Presidential scheduling. Secretary Baker was to have taken one of the hours to brief on the Moscow Ministerial. Due to schedule difficulties, however, the Secretary will brief the President on May 22. That leaves us with an extra session for briefing the President on the Summit, should we choose to use it, and the President agrees. We suggest using the extra session during the last briefing time to summarize major issues and objectives for the Summit. We propose the following briefings and outside participants (we have not extended invitations to any of the outside participants pending your review of the schedule)

documentCandidates.documentTitle: Twenty-Eighth Semiannual Report to the CSCE Commission
documentCandidates.context: Washington, D.C. 20520 May 18, 1990 MEMORANDUM FOR BRENT SCOWCROFT THE WHITE HOUSE SUBJECT: Twenty-Eighth Semiannual Report to the CSCE Commission Public Law 94-304 requires the President to submit a report semiannually to the Congressional CSCE Commission on the implementation of the Helsinki Final Act. The attached draft report, which covers the period October 1, 1989 - March 31, 1990, is due to the Commission on June 4. The report provides a factual survey of developments in the areas covered by the Helsinki Final Act and the Madrid and Vienna Concluding Documents: human rights and humanitarian concerns; security; economic, scientific, and technological cooperation; and educational and cultural exchanges. It concentrates on compliance with the Final Act and the Concluding Documents by the Soviet Union and Eastern Europe. The Department recommends that the Secretary of State be

documentCandidates.documentTitle: Appointment with the President for Council of Europe
documentCandidates.context: 6293 August 3, 1990 MEMORANDUM FOR BRENT SCOWCROFT THE WHITE HOUSE SUBJECT: Appointment with the President for Council of Europe Secretary General Catherine Lalumiere Lalumiere plans to visit the United States during the fall. She has asked to call on the President and has proposed September 24, October 18-19, and October 24-25 as possible dates for such a call. The Council of Europe (COE) is a regional organization consisting of the 23 West European democracies. East European countries are now applying for membership -- which would require that they sign the COE's European Convention on Human Rights and accept the jurisdiction of the European Court of Human Rights in Strasbourg. The COE's Parliamentary Assembly will also provide a training ground and contact point for the new Eastern European parliamentarians. The Soviet Union holds "special observer" status in the COE and

documentCandidates.context: August 16, 1991 UNCLASSIFIED MEMORANDUM FOR BRENT SCOWCROFT THE WHITE HOUSE Subject: Foreign Travel by Secretary Lujan to the USSR, August 31 - September 9 Secretary Lujan will travel to the USSR for a meeting with the Ministry of Metallurgy. He is tentatively scheduled to depart for the Moscow on August 31 and return to Washington on September 9. The Department has no objection to the proposed travel. R.A.LAMANTIL L W. documentCandidates.documentTitle: Ratification of the Agreement between the United
documentCandidates.context: Washington, D.C. 20520 October 16, 1991 MEMORANDUM FOR BRENT SCOWCROFT THE WHITE HOUSE Subject: Ratification of the Agreement between the United States and the Union of Soviet Socialist Republics on the Maritime Boundary, with Annex Attached for signature by the President is the instrument of ratification, in duplicate, of the Agreement between the United States of America and the Union of Soviet Socialist Republics on the Maritime Boundary, with Annex, signed at Washington June 1, 1990. The Senate gave its advice and consent to ratification of the Agreement on September 16, 1991. The Agreement establishes the maritime boundary between the United States and the Soviet Union in the North Pacific Ocean, Bering and Chukchi Seas and the Arctic Ocean. The Agreement resolves differences between the two countries over where they may exercise jurisdiction, in accordance with international

documentCandidates.context: (With CONFIDENTIAL Attachment) December 4, 1991 MEMORANDUM FOR BRENT SCOWCROFT THE WHITE HOUSE SUBJECT: Appointment of Stasys Lozoraitis of the Republic of Lithuania as Ambassador to the United States The Government of the Republic of Lithuania has inquired whether our Government agrees to the appointment of Stasys Lozoraitis as Ambassador Extraordinary and Plenipotentiary of Lithuania to the United States (TAB 2). documentCandidates.documentTitle: Presidential meeting with Bulgarian PM Filip Dimitrov
documentCandidates.context: January 15, 1992 DECL: OADR MEMORANDUM FOR BRENT SCOWCROFT THE WHITE HOUSE Subject: Presidential meeting with Bulgarian PM Filip Dimitrov The Department requests that the President meet with Bulgarian Prime Minister Dimitrov for 30 minutes sometime in the first week of March, during a private visit Dimitrov is planning at the invitation of the International Republican Institute (formerly NRI). Once the USSR's closest ally, Bulgaria now has one of the most enlightened democratic governments among the ex-Communist states. Its policies of ethnic tolerance, rapprochement with former adversary Turkey, and restraint toward Macedonia are helping shore up the region against spillover from Yugoslavia's civil war. It has borne sizable risks to forge ahead with an active program of market reforms and economic stabilization. In the past year it has been a steadfast supporter of U.S.

documentCandidates.documentTitle: Appointment of Alim Shakirovich Djamburshin of
documentCandidates.context: UNCLASSIFIED (With SECRET Attachment) MEMORANDUM FOR BRENT SCOWCROFT THE WHITE HOUSE SUBJECT: Appointment of Alim Shakirovich Djamburshin of the Republic of Kazakhstan as Ambassador to the United States The Government of the Republic of Kazakhstan has inquired whether our Government agrees to the appointment of Alim Shakirovich Djamburshin as Ambassador Extraordinary and Plenipotentiary of Kazakhstan to the United States (TAB 2). A biography of Mr. Djamburshin is attached (TAB 1). The Department believes from the information available that Mr. Djamburshin will make a good Ambassador to the United States and recommends that the President agree to the proposed appointment. If he concurs, the Department will inform the Government of the Republic of Kazakhstan. R.A.LAMANTAM

documentCandidates.documentTitle: SOVIET SEMINARIANS' SCHOLARSHIP PROGRAM NAMED FOR MRS.
documentCandidates.context: TO: SUSAN PORTER ROSE FROM: CONDOLEEZZA RICE CR SUBJECT: SOVIET SEMINARIANS' SCHOLARSHIP PROGRAM NAMED FOR MRS. BUSH The State Department has no objection to this activity, per se, but points out (I think correctly) that there are numerous divisions and animosities among religious groups in the Soviet Union and their emigre counterparts in the United States. These divisions make it difficult to establish a sufficiently ecumenical scholarship to satisfy all involved. We could find ourselves in the embarrassing position of having some group claim that the scholarship is discriminatory. The risks are significant since we would likely have little control over the program. I also talked with Nick Rostow of the NSC legal staff who says that he believes that Mrs. Bush should not lend her name to the

Background As a result of the World Health Organization (WHO) Smallpox Eradication Program, which included significant United States participation, the last case of smallpox in the world occurred in 1977. My host will be Dr. Oleg Shishkin, the Soviet Minister of General Machine Building. Dr. Shishkin is the official responsible for the Soviet space program. At his invitation, I will visit a number of Ministry facilities to learn more about the Soviet space capabilities and plans. In accordance with the referenced memorandum, your approval is requested. Richard H. Truly AMBASBADOR OF THE

documentCandidates.documentTitle: Proposed Agreements for Cooperation with Hungary and
documentCandidates.context: SECURITY AFFAIRS MEMORANDUM FOR DEPUTY ASSISTANT SECRETARY, NUCLEAR ENERGY AND ENERGY TECHNOLOGY AFFAIRS, OES/N, DEPARTMENT OF STATE SUBJECT: Proposed Agreements for Cooperation with Hungary and Czechoslovakia Concerning Peaceful Uses of Nuclear Energy This is in response to the State/OES memoranda of 1 October and 15 October soliciting agency comments on the proposed subject agreements. DOD has reviewed the agreements both from a non- proliferation and an East-West technology transfer perspective and do not object to their content. We do believe strongly, however, that the U.S. Government should not move forward with any peaceful nuclear cooperation with these countries until the U.S. has clear evidence that they both have established export control systems to protect the transfer of dual-use, nuclear-related technologies. documentCandidates.context: OMB LRD/RDI 14:22 26/61/80 MEMO TO ALL AGENCIES SUBJECT: Position Papers for the FREEDOM Support Bill, 8. 2532 The State Department will be drafting a letter to the conferees on S. 2532. Attached to State's letter will be position papers on issues of concern to the Administration. An initial list of these issues is attached to this referral. There may be additional items that we may want to highlight as well. In addition, the Justice Department has been asked to address constitutional concerns in a separate letter to the conferees. The attached list identifies the lead agency, who should draft the Administration's position on the issue. If a second agency is identified next to an issue, the lead agency needs to coordinate the position paper with that agency prior to submission to OMB. (U) THE SOVIET UNION MADE CLEAR THAT THEY WISHED THE GERMAN PROCESS TO CONNECT WITH DISARMAMENT, INTO WHICH THE FORCES OF THE FRG AND THEN THE UNITED GERMANY COULD BE BROUGHT. THERE WAS CONCERN THAT IF U.S. AND SOVIET TROOPS IN CENTRAL EUROPE WERE BROUGHT DOWN TO 195,000 MEN OR LESS, THE GERMAN FORCES WOULD BE STRONGER THAN THOSE OF THE TWO SUPER POWERS IN THE CORE OF EUROPE. 13. (U) SOVIET CONCERNS REGARDING THE STATIONING OF SOVIET TROOPS ARE NOT PREDOMINANTLY ABOUT HOW LONG THEY CAN STAY. RATHER, THEY DEPEND ON OTHER CONSIDERATIONS, SUCH AS LOGISTICAL ONES. documentCandidates.documentTitle: Travel Itinerary for Kevin R. Callwood,
documentCandidates.context: for National Security Affairs FROM : James Executive D. Berg Vice familing President SUBJECT: Travel Itinerary for Kevin R. Callwood, Vice President for Corporate Affairs Mr. Kevin R. Callwood, Vice President for Corporate Affairs, Overseas Private Investment Corporation (OPIC), will be on official travel to Lisbon, Portugal and Paris, France from November 7 - 16, 1990. During the period, November 8 - 9, 1990, Mr. Callwood will participate in meetings with officials of the government of Portugal as well as private sector individuals. The purpose of this trip is to gather information for a possible investment mission to Portugal. Mr. Callwood is also participating in the Department of Commerce, U.S. & Foreign Commercial Service, Paris Symposium on Eastern Europe scheduled for November 13 - 15, 1990.

documentCandidates.documentTitle: SUMMARY: UNITED BERLIN'S GOVERNING MAYOR, EBERHARD
documentCandidates.context: PROBLEMS TO BONN. REFS: (A) BERLIN 191; (B) BERLIN 518 WARD 1. ENTIRE TEXT. 2. SUMMARY: UNITED BERLIN'S GOVERNING MAYOR, EBERHARD DIEPGEN (CDU), WOULD LIKE TO MAKE HIS FIRST MAJOR FOREIGN TRIP TO WASHINGTON. AFTER BEING PRESSED BY THE SOVIETS, HE PLANS TO TRAVEL TO MOSCOW MARCH 26-28. BE WOULD CARRY TO THE PRESIDENT AND SECRETARY BERLIN'S SPECIAL THANKS FOR OUR STEADFAST SUPPORT FOR THE CITY'S FREEDOM AND UNITY AS WELL AS A MESSAGE OF BERLINERS' AND GERMANS' SOLIDARITY ON THE GULF. HIS PLAN HAS CHANCELLOR KOHL'S SUPPORT. A DIEPGEN VISIT WOULD ALSO PROVIDE U.S. ICYMAKERS WITH A FIRST HAND ACCOUNT OF THE PROBLEMS IN MELDING THE OLD AND NEW GERMAN STATES. IF DIEPGEN'S TIMING FITS (PARA 3), I WOULD SUGGEST A BRIEF PHOTO OPPORTUNITY WITH THE PRESIDENT AND A LONGER CONVERSATION WITH THE SECRETARY AS WELL AS OTHER MEETINGS IN THE DEPARTMENT. THE GERMAN EMBASSY

documentCandidates.documentTitle: Transmittal to the Congress of the
documentCandidates.context: NATIONAL SECURITY AFFAIRS FROM: Ambassador Carla A. Hills CAST SUBJECT: Transmittal to the Congress of the U.S. - Soviet Trade Agreement You have requested my views on whether the Administration should now transmit the Trade Agreement to Congress for approval. I believe we should transmit the Agreement after the following steps have been taken: 1. Assurances from the Soviets on intellectual property (IPR) commitments. Legislation recently passed by the Soviet Union is inconsistent with IPR commitments they have made in the Trade Agreement. Further, pending Soviet legislation raises additional serious questions on compliance. Unless we receive reliable assurances that the Soviets intend to fix these laws, the copyright industries are almost certain to be vocal opponents during the approval process. I plan to call in the Soviet
